clean_story:
Silent tools. Adaptation over strength.
Ninja means "one who endures"—fitting for weapons built to outlast detection, not brute force.

Early ninja repurposed farming tools: kama (sickles) became slashing weapons; kunai (digging spikes) turned into climbing hooks or thrown distractors. Like a crowbar used as both lever and club, simplicity hid intent. By the 15th century, shinobi shozoku (ninja gear) included makibishi (spiked caltrops)—scattered like Lego pieces on a dark floor—to slow pursuers without a sound.

The shuriken evolved from carpentry nails, flattened for aerodynamics. A 17th-century manual, Bansenshukai, details how blacksmiths folded iron into star-shaped blades that spun true when thrown sidearm—like skipping stones, but lethal. Smoke bombs (enmaku) used gunpowder wrapped in egg membranes; the thin skin burned fast, creating instant cover.

Three related subjects are feudal espionage tools, metallurgy in covert warfare, and how terrain shaped stealth.
